course_description: This course examines the philosophy of Kant & his influence on the development of European philosophy in the 19th & 20th centuries. Kant's philosophy is pivotal for the subsequent developments of Idealism, Marxism, Existentialism & Phenomenology, & analytic philosophy. The course will be of interest to all students who want to deepen their understanding of the origins of some of the central issues in modern philosophy. This course may not run if there are fewer than 20 enrolments.

course_aims: The course examines Kant's main philosophical ideas, including his metaphysics, his ethics and political philosophy, and his philosophy of history and at the same time aims to develop these skills and capacities:

learning_outcomes.description: Understand some key philosophical concepts and identify the major ideas in Kant.
Demonstrate effective communication skills in the contexts of group discussion and essay writing.
Demonstrate a capacity for independent and creative thought.
Use critical judgment to solve philosophical problems and issues.
Engage ethically with ideas and experiences beyond their own immediate social and personal context.

assessment_details.title: Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Tutorials commence in the second week of semester and are compulsory for the course. Students should participate in the 10 tutorials in order to be eligible to receive full marks for this component (10%). Weekly reading for the tutorials is required. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted us of Generative AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: First Essay
assessment_details.task_description: First essay questions cover the topics from weeks 1-6. Students are expected to answer only ONE of these questions for the essay. Word length is 1600 words. This assessment task evaluates student’s abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit your essay via Turn-in-it on the class site.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 12 (10 Mar - 25 May)
learning_activities.topic: Tutorials Tutorials are closely tied to lecture material and are designed to allow students to focus on relevant themes. Weekly required readings allow close and careful analysis of emerging themes. Tutorials commence in the second week of semester and are compulsory for the course. Students will receive a combined participation mark based on regular submitted reflections: 10% There are ten tutorials during the semester.
Lecture The metaphysical deduction, identifying the categories' A50/B74-A84/B116.
Lecture Transcendental deduction A. In italics on the bottom half of the page in the textbook. A84-A130.
Lecture Transcendental Deduction B. In normal typeface on the top half of the page of the textbook. B130-B169.
Lecture The schematism, axioms, the first and second analogies. A131/B170-A147/B187; A158/B219=A211/B256.
Lecture Third analogy and postulates. Refutation of idealism; Phenomena and noumena. A212/B257-A235/B287;A236/B295-A260/B315.
Lecture Antinomies of reason; regulative ideas. A293/B350-A320/B377.
Lecture Groundwork of the metaphysics of morals.
Lecture Metaphysics of Morals - Doctrine of Right.
Lecture Metaphysics of Morals, 'Doctrine of Virtue': Kant on love, respect, and friendship
Lecture Kant's Cosmopolitanism and critique of colonialism.
